text: Stem pads are placed on the right scapula and in the left inframammary fold anterior to the anterior axillary line.
A spinal needle is used to localize with great precision the trajectory and location of each of the additional ports.
The middle port is at the cephalad edge of the hilum, just anterior to the projected line of pericardiotomy.
The upper lateral port is placed so as to produce a gentle trajectory over the superior vena cava into the aortic root.
A fourth port is placed directly over the aortic root, immediately lateral to the mammary bundle.
The initial pericardiotomy is performed anterior to the phrenic nerve, caudal to the pulmonary hilum.
Two graspers are used to elevate a fold of pericardium before the incision is made so as to maximize safety.
The harmonic scalpel is used to continue the pericardiotomy to a point just caudal to the oblique sinus, approximately two centimeters anterior to the right phrenic nerve.
Note that the diaphragm is being retracted with an endokitner placed through the medial port.
Cephalad, the pericardiotomy, continues onto the superior vena cava reflection.
In case of extensive pericardial fat, it is helpful to divide the fat before the pericardium so as to maximize safe identification of the underlying structures.

It is necessary to continue the pericardiotomy fairly cephalad so as to enable visualization of the right pulmonary artery medial to the superior vena cava when the camera is placed in the upper lateral port.
A window of pericardium is then taken out overlying the aortic root.
This allows visualization of the aortic root itself when the camera, again, is placed in the upper lateral port.
Retraction sutures are placed over the right superior pulmonary vein and over the oblique sinus.
We begin with an initial mobilization of the superior vena cava up to the azicus vein.
Note that the medial retraction of the superior vena cava with the assistant using a Kettner in the upper medial port facilitates this maneuver.
Precise localization of the phrenic nerve is kept in mind at all times.
Once the azagus is identified, an additional retraction stitch is frequently helpful.
Immediately cephalad to the previously placed one.
The muscular sheath overlying the right pulmonary artery is divided sharply and the dissection continued with a harmonic.
Meticulous dissection here facilitates the subsequent encircling of the superior vena cava.
The thoracoscope is subsequently placed in the upper lateral port to enable visualization of the right pulmonary artery medial to the superior vena cava.

The investing sheath surrounding the right pulmonary artery is divided sharply, as shown, and the lumbotip dissector, which is placed through the medial port, is used to circumnavigate the superior vena cava.
blunt dissection then delivers the glide path plastic sheath through the defect previously created in the adventitious surrounding the right pulmonary artery.
At this point, the camera is placed in the lower port so as to enable precise localization of a point on the chest wall directly anterior to the aesagus SVC junction.
Again, a spinal needle is used to localize this point, which is immediately lateral to the mammary bundle, as shown.
This enables elevation of the superior vena cava and clear identification of the appropriate plane of dissection to enter the transverse sinus.
An endokitner retracts the dome in its caudate direction so as to enable visualization of the appropriate tissue planes.
At this point, the thick muscular sheath, which intervenes between the right pulmonary artery and dome of the left atrium, is clearly visible.
Because of its vascularity, it's important to use the harmonic to divide it.
This preliminary dissection of the right pulmonary artery away from the dome of the left atrium also facilitates subsequent circumnavigation of the right pulmonary hilum.
We proceed now to the oblique sinus where blunt dissection opens up the areolar tissue between the right inferior pulmonary vein on the left and the inferior vena cava.
In cases where the oblique sinus tissue is rather thick, it is important to identify the right inferior pulmonary vein first and hug the outer aspect of the
inferior pulmonary vein margin so as to avoid inadvertent injury to the inferior vena cava.
It's frequently possible to visualize the distal coronary sinus from the oblique sinus.

In encircling the right pulmonary antrum, it is helpful to use two kitteners, one retracting the right pulmonary artery in a northward direction and a second retracting the dome of the left atrium in a southward direction.
So it opens up a large landing area for the lumbotip dissector.
We next proceed with high frequency stimulation of the interatrial groove to test for the epicardial autonomics.
positive responses are assessed with the usual bradycardia.
The right pulmonary antral isolation is performed using multiple applications of this quadripolar rate of frequency clamp.
It is helpful to place as much of the interatrial groove fat into the clamp so as to maximize ablation of the epicardial autonomics.
Multiple ablations are performed in different sites so as to maximize the isolation effect.
Bidirectional block confirms effective isolation of both pulmonary veins.
At this point, we return to the transfer sinus to complete the dissection of the left atrial dome.
The defect created in the adventitia overlying the dome of the left atrium from the luma tip dissector is used as a guide point to enter the transverse sinus dissection.

With northward retraction of the right pulmonary artery and southward retraction of the dome of the left atrium, the dissection proceeds in a lateral to medial direction with eventual division of the ligament of Marshall.
This dissection is carried all the way over to the left superior pulmonary vein and its pericardial reflection.

It is vitally important to complete this dissection with great meticulousness in order to assure that the subsequent ablations across the dome are actually on atrial myocardium and not the overlying areolar fat.
This assures consistent transmorality of the ablations across the dome.
At this point, the left superior pulmonary vein is clearly visualized along with its pericardial reflection.
Note that the hot anvil of the harmonic scalpel is rotated slightly away from the heart so as to avoid thermal injury.
At this point, the dissection clearly has extended all the way over to the left superior pulmonary vein pericardial reflection.
We next proceed with a defatting of the dome of the left atrium, again, to maximize transmorality of the subsequent ablations.
For this, I picked the absolute porterhouse of left atrias as far as overlying and vesting fat is concerned.
The dissection begins just over the right superior pulmonary vein.
The sweeping motion with the harmonic scalpel allows identification of the appropriate tissue plane.
The dissection proceeds lateral to medial and from anterior to posterior.
As the dissection proceeds posteriorly, the retracting endokitner on the dome of the left atrium is placed more posteriorly accordingly.
Because of the friability of the tissue, only minimal torque is applied with the grasper, just enough to identify the appropriate dissection plane.
Again, the hot anvil of the harmonic scalpel is rotated so as to avoid contact with the heart.
Because of the vascularity of this tissue, it is absolutely necessary to use the harmonic for this dissection.
One can easily imagine in this patient with the thickness of the epicardial fat that in the absence of this maneuver of defatting the dome, any ablations would be worthless.
It is therefore a critical step to be performed prior to the application of any ablations.
The completed dissection results in bare myocardium that completely exposes the dome of the left atrium from left superior pulmonary vein to right superior pulmonary vein.
The posterior border is clearly the posterior pericardium itself.
This depicts the triangle of ablations which will be created on the dome at the left atrium.
We proceed first with the creation of the transmicroline.
With the camera placed in the upper lateral port, the ablation probe is placed with the medial port onto the aortic root.
The probe is placed at the left non-coronary commissure, immediately lateral to the left main coronary artery.
Placement on the anterior mitral trigone is verified with real-time intraoperative transversal echocardiography.
After the initial ablation is performed at the aortic root, we proceed down to the left superior pulmonary vein immediately adjacent to the pericardial reflection.
Again, multiple ablations are performed at each point so as to assure trans-morality.
The completed transmicroline connects the two points with multiply redundant ablations.
The camera is kept in the upper lateral port.
The over-under move allows the initiation of the hypotenuse line to be localized precisely at the apex of the previously created transmartial line.
Then the superior vena cava is elevated in conventional fashion and the line extended onto the right superior pulmonary vein.
Note that the line intersects the previously created right pulmonary vein isolation box.
The dome is retracted and the previously created two lines are easily visualized.
The so-called roof line or superior interantral line is extended from the left superior pulmonary vein at its pericardial reflection onto the right superior pulmonary vein.
This results in the creation of an enclosed triangle on the dome of the left atrium, which incorporates the connecting ablations to the anterior mitral trigone.
Verification of the integrity of all of the constituent lines is confirmed with the demonstration of bidirectional block using a probe placed on untreated atrium in the center of the triangle.
We next proceed with the isolation of the superior vena cava using the left-sided clamp
at a point approximately two centimeters proximal to the sinoatrial node at the SVC-RA junction.
Again, bidirectional block is confirmed.
We proceed back to the oblique sinus to ablate the autonomic bearing area between the right inferior pulmonary vein and the inferior vena cava.
If the coronary sinus is visualized through the oblique sinus, then the distal coronary sinus is also ablated extensively.
Again, multiply redundant ablations which overlap and essentially neutralize the entire autonomic bearing area are performed.
Once this is completed, repeat high frequency autonomic testing is performed to verify extinction of all previously positive autonomic ganglia.
The distal cornea sinus, which has been completely ablated, is shown here.
This completes the right chest portion of the operation.
We proceed with intercostal nerve blocks at each of the port site inner spaces using a reusable liquid nitrogen cryoprobe.
The applications are at negative 30 degrees for 60 seconds.
This provides approximately three weeks of chest wall analgesia.

After placement of the initial port at the mid axillary line, again in approximately the fifth intercostal space, the spinal needle is used to place the next port at the caudal edge of the hilum to enable subsequent visualization of the floor.

And finally, the last port on the left side is placed directly over the left superior pulmonary vein so as to enable as flat an angle as possible for subsequent retraction of the left atrial appendage.
The pericardiotomy is begun posterior to the left phrenic nerve.
Again, caudal to the hilum where the pericardium is most lax.
the pericardiotomy is continued posteriorly towards the descending aorta.
Cephalad, the pericardiotomy is continued onto the left pulmonary artery of reflection.
Notice that a maximal margin of pericardium is left posterior to the left phrenic nerve so as to avoid traction injury.
Retraction sutures are subsequently placed immediately caudal to the left inferior pulmonary vein on the posterior leaf and directly over the left atrial appendage on the anterior leaf.
This second retraction suture is brought out through the medial port itself.
Typically, the ligament of Marshall has been divided from the dissection previously performed through the right chest.
Any residual ligament tissue should be divided either with scissors or the harmonic scalpel.
Note the ablations which are easily visible on the dome of the left atrium from the right chest.
Division of the ligament of Marshall is important for purposes of autonomic denervation as well as to facilitate subsequent circumnavigation of the left pulmonary antrum.
With the dome dissection having previously completely separated the dome itself from the left pulmonary artery, circumnavigation of the hilum is quite easy.
High frequency autonomic testing is optional on this side since the previously performed dissection generally results in negative autonomic responses.
The left-sided clamp is passed around the pulmonary antrum.
And again, multiple ablations are performed.
In cases where the left atrial appendage is large, it may be retracted using an endoscopic fan retractor.
I generally use a five millimeter reusable fan retractor for this purpose.
After multiple ablations have been performed to isolate the left pulmonary veins, bidirectional block is confirmed across both the superior and inferior pulmonary veins.
We now use the bipolar linear ablation probe to continue the line of ablations which were previously placed on the dome of the left atrium from the right chest onto the left superior pulmonary vein.
This is vitally important to assure that there are no breaks and thus potential for flutter circuits on the left atrial isthmus.

Using a 12 millimeter large five fingered endoscopic fan retractor, the apex is elevated, resulting in clear visualization of the floor of the left atrium, including the inferior vena cava, oblique sinus, and right inferior pulmonary vein.
It is therefore very easy to create a line of ablations across the floor connecting the two inferior pulmonary veins.

This results in a four-sided box isolating the entire posterior left atrium, and the integrity of the constituent ablations is confirmed again with a demonstration of bidirectional block using a probe placed on untreated atrium in the center of the box.
We finally proceed with ligation of the left atrial appendage using the interclip device.
It is important to identify the appropriate axis that the base of the left atrial appendage makes vis-a-vis the chest wall and bend the interclip shaft appropriately.
The distal tip of the appendage is gently manipulated into the aperture of the clip itself using an atraumatic clump grasper.
Note that excessive traction on the appendage, which obviously is quite friable, is avoided
Once the body of the appendage is delivered into the device, the device itself is deployed.
While the device is deployed, it is helpful to apply gentle traction on the appendage so as to maximize inclusion of the appendage in the clip.
The retaining sutures are divided so as to avoid excessive traction on the appendage when the frame is removed.
The advantage of the atria clip over staples
consists twofold, first in avoidance of recanalization issues, but probably more importantly is the electrical isolation of potentially arrhythmogenic substrate in the appendage itself.
